speaker: ALEX
text: This is Git Going with GitHub, episode 42: Accessing Workshop Materials. I am Alex. By the end of this episode, Accessing Workshop Materials should feel less like a wall of GitHub words and more like a set of moves you can trust.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I am here for the practical questions: what should I listen for, what can go wrong, and what is the next calm move?

speaker: ALEX
text: Today we are working on this: Downloading the repository, reading offline, keeping updated, audio format. I want the learner to leave with a mental map, not just a remembered path through buttons.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the goal is understanding first, then action, then confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. We are building a rhythm: orient yourself, take one intentional action, then verify what changed before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to Get, Read, and Keep These Documents: Why this appendix exists: The workshop content is available in multiple formats - Markdown source files, pre-built HTML pages, and a live GitHub Pages site. The next useful detail is concrete: This guide explains how to access each format, download materials for offline use, and keep your copy up to date.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Learning Cards: Accessing Workshop Materials has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. The GitHub Pages site includes skip-to-content links and ARIA landmarks -- press D (NVDA) or R (JAWS) to jump to the main content landmark on any page. On GitHub.com, press T on the repository's main page to open the file finder -- type any filename to jump directly to it without navigating the file tree. After cloning the repository, open it in VS Code and use the Explorer panel (Ctrl+Shift+E) to browse the file tree with arrow keys. The HTML version in the html/ folder provides the most polished reading experience with styled, navigable pages that work offline in any browser. For reading Markdown source files, use VS Code's built-in preview (Ctrl+Shift+V) which renders headings, links, and code blocks in a formatted view. When downloading a ZIP from GitHub, the Code button and its dropdown appear near the top of the repository page -- look for the green button.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 1. Browsing Online (GitHub Pages): if the facilitator has enabled GitHub Pages for this repository, the workshop materials are available as a website at a URL like. That matters in practice: Your facilitator will share the exact URL.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Open the URL in your browser; The landing page (index.html) is the workshop homepage - equivalent to the README; Use headings (H key in NVDA/JAWS browse mode) to navigate within any page; and All internal links between chapters and appendices work - click any cross-reference to go directly to that page. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 1 checks: Bookmark the URL for quick access during the workshop. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on 2. Reading on GitHub.com. You can read every file directly on GitHub.com without downloading anything.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Go to the repository page (your facilitator will share the link); The README renders automatically as the repository homepage; Click into the docs/ folder to see all chapters and appendices; and Click any.md file - GitHub renders it as formatted text with headings, links, and code blocks. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Navigating the repository with a screen reader has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. File list: The repository file listing is a grid. Use T to jump to the file table, then arrow keys to navigate rows. File content: Once inside a file, GitHub renders the Markdown. Use H to navigate headings. Breadcrumbs: At the top of each file view, breadcrumb links show the path (e.g., Learning-Room / docs / 06-working-with-pull-requests.md). Use these to navigate back. Go to File shortcut: Press T on the repository's main page to open the file finder - type any filename to jump to it.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat Option A: Clone with Git (recommended) as decoration. Cloning gives you a full copy of the repository that you can update later with git pull. That is not trivia. After cloning, every file (Markdown source, HTML output, scripts, learning-room materials) is on your computer. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Clone the repository; git clone https://github.com/community-access/git-going-with-github.git; Move into the folder; cd Learning-Room. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Option B: Download ZIP (no Git required) is still useful because if you do not have Git installed or prefer not to use the command line.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Go to the repository page on GitHub.com. Then: Press T to find the file finder, or navigate to the green Code button (it is a dropdown button near the top of the file listing). Next: Activate the Code button - a dropdown menu opens. Last: Choose Download ZIP. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Save the file and extract it to a folder on your computer. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Put Screen reader navigation for the Code dropdown into plain language. A ZIP download is a snapshot. The useful version is: It does not update automatically - see Section 7 for how to get updates.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. The Code button is near the repository description, after the branch selector. In NVDA/JAWS browse mode, press B to jump between buttons until you reach "Code". Activate it with Enter or Space. The dropdown contains options including "Download ZIP" - arrow down to find it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. To download a single file (for example, one chapter or the screen reader cheat sheet).

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on From GitHub.com. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because for HTML files: Navigate to the html/ folder and download the.html version of any file using the same Raw → Save method. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is navigate to the file in the repository. Step two is click the Raw button (it appears above the file content, in the toolbar with "Preview", "Code", "Blame" buttons). Step three is the browser shows the raw file content. Step four is press Ctrl+S (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+S (macOS) to save the page. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: From a cloned repository: If you have already cloned the repo, every file is already on your computer. The next useful detail is concrete: Open the folder and copy whichever files you need.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Here is the learner-facing version. Once you have downloaded or cloned the repository, you can read everything offline.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Reading Markdown files: they are plain text with lightweight formatting syntax. That matters in practice: VS Code renders Markdown with a preview panel (Ctrl+Shift+V).

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Reading HTML files. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Reading HTML files. The HTML files work the same offline as online. This is the part to say slowly: All ARIA landmarks, heading structure, and skip links are embedded in each file. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Open html/index.html in any web browser; All internal links between pages work locally - no internet connection required; and Navigate between chapters using the links at the bottom of each page. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the pre-flight check here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Recommended offline reading setup has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. VS Code with Markdown: Open the repo folder in VS Code, browse the Explorer, and read files directly in the editor. Use Ctrl+Shift+V to open the rendered preview. Browser with HTML: Open html/index.html and navigate from there - this gives the most polished reading experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should happen before anyone copies and runs it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat If you cloned with Git as decoration. If you have not made local changes, this updates your copy cleanly. That is not trivia. If you have local edits, Git will attempt to merge - see Chapter 7 - Merge Conflicts if needed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Navigate to your local copy; cd Learning-Room; Pull the latest changes; git pull. npm run build:html. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, If you downloaded a ZIP is still useful because download a fresh ZIP from the repository and replace your local copy. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: There is no incremental update for ZIP downloads.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What decision is this helping them make?

speaker: ALEX
text: 8. Which Format Should I Use? has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the comparison to make a decision, not to recite a table. The main contrasts are: Reading during the workshop means GitHub Pages (online HTML) means No setup, always current, fully accessible. Offline reference means Local HTML (html/ folder) means Works in any browser, no internet needed, styled and navigable. Editing or contributing means Markdown (.md files) means The source format - edit these, then rebuild HTML.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Choosing the Right Format. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Next: Appendix Z: GitHub Skills Back: Appendix X: Resources Teaching chapter: Chapter 00: Pre-Workshop Setup.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. For workshop use, the GitHub Pages HTML site is the most accessible option -- it includes skip links, landmarks, and proper heading structure in every page. For offline reference, the local HTML files in the html/ folder work identically to the online version with full navigation between pages. For contributing edits, work with the Markdown source files in docs/ using VS Code -- these are the canonical source that generates everything else. GitHub Pages and local HTML both support browser zoom to 200%+ without layout breakage -- use whichever gives you a better reading experience. VS Code's Markdown preview inherits your editor theme -- if you use a high-contrast theme, the preview will match. If viewing on GitHub.com, enable GitHub's dark theme (Settings then Appearance) for reduced eye strain during long reading sessions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 42. Next in the series is episode 43, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
